corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: We report state-of-the-art results that out-perform heavily engineered hybrid systems.
In this section, we review LAS networks and introduce some notation to parameterize them.
We also introduce the learning rate schedules we use to train the networks, as they turn out to be an important factor in determining performance.
For LibriSpeech, we use a two-layer RNN with embedding dimension 1024 used in for the LM, which is trained on the LibriSpeech LM corpus.
For Switchboard, we use a two-layer RNN with embedding dimension 256, which is trained on the combined transcripts of the Fisher and Switchboard datasets.
We achieve state-of-the-art performance on the LibriSpeech 960h and Swichboard 300h tasks, outperforming all prior work.

For Switchboard, we achieve 7.2%/14.6% on the Switchboard/CallHome portion of the Hub5'00 test set without the use of a language model, and 6.8%/14.1% with shallow fusion, which compares to the previous state-of-the-art hybrid system at 8.3%/17.3% WER.
More generally, learned augmentation techniques have explored different sequences of augmentation transformations that have achieved state-of-the-art performance in the image domain .

Inspired by the recent success of augmentation in the speech and vision domains, we propose SpecAugment, an augmentation method that operates on the log mel spectrogram of the input audio, rather than the raw audio itself.

This approach while rudimentary, is remarkably effective and allows us to train end-to-end ASR networks, called Listen Attend and Spell (LAS) , to surpass more complicated hybrid systems, and achieve state-of-the-art results even without the use of Language Models (LMs).

Upon shallow fusion with an LM trained on the LibriSpeech LM corpus, we are able to better our performance (2.5% WER on test-clean and 5.8% WER on testother), improving the current state of the art on test-other by 22% relatively.

Upon shallow fusion with an LM trained on the LibriSpeech LM corpus, we are able to better our performance (2.5% WER on test-clean and 5.8% WER on testother), improving the current state of the art on test-other by 22% relatively.

Upon shallow fusion with an LM trained on the LibriSpeech LM corpus, we are able to better our performance (2.5% WER on test-clean and 5.8% WER on testother), improving the current state of the art on test-other by 22% relatively.
Upon shallow fusion with an LM trained on the combined transcript of the Switchboard and Fisher (LDC200{4,5}T19) corpora, we obtain 6.8%/14.1% WER on the Switchboard/Callhome portion.
For the Switchboard 300h task, transcripts from the training set are combined with those of the Fisher corpus to construct the WPM.
While we are able to get state-of-the-art results with augmentation, we can get further improvements by using a language model.
For LibriSpeech, we use a two-layer RNN with embedding dimension 1024 used in for the LM, which is trained on the LibriSpeech LM corpus.
In Table , we present three training results for which time warping, time masking and frequency masking have been turned off, respectively.

As can be observed from the training curves of the networks in Figure , the networks during training not only under-fit the loss and WER on the augmented training set, but also on the training set itself when trained on augmented data.

We are able to obtain state-of-the-art results on the Lib-riSpeech 960h and Switchboard 300h tasks on end-to-end LAS networks by augmenting the training set using simple handcrafted policies, surpassing the performance of hybrid systems even without the aid of a language model.
We present SpecAugment, a simple data augmentation method for speech recognition.
